nt: Four floors, best sound system in Tokyo. The techno institution since 2000.
Basement techno. No phones on the dancefloor. Serious crowd.
Underground electronic. Reliable lineup, less tourist traffic than Womb.
Multi-room Shibuya club. Best for late-night weekends when it warms up.
Shibuya staple. J-pop and Y2K energy. Always packed on weekends.
Massive Shinkiba club — outdoor pool, three rooms, the one for a real night.
Glittery, chaotic, completely unhinged. Exactly what it sounds like.
Kabukicho techno and drum and bass. Rougher crowd, better music.
Tight basement space. Good for when you want the small-room intensity.
朝までパック at multiple chains 5 min walk. ¥1500-2500 all-you-can-drink until 5am.
Big Echo east exit, Karaoke-kan. Deep catalog, deep night.
Several 24hr chains near the crossing. Pick the brightest sign.
Strange ambient energy at 3am in Akiba. The crowd is committed.
Bar karaoke not booth — everyone sings together. Completely different energy.
The legendary one since the 1960s. One room has a full working carousel.
Bali-themed — fake tropical plants, bamboo, mood lighting.
Wedding-themed. Champagne tower in the bathroom. Baroque excess.
ラブホテル街 8 min walk. Some rooms unrenovated since the 80s.
Highest concentration in Japan. All night, all themed. Pick one at random.
Dense cluster behind Shibuya. Walk uphill from the crossing.
Solo booths, spice slider, 24hr. Perfect solitary 4am ramen.
Same. Nobody talks to you. You will be healed.
Post-club standard. 24hr.
Family restaurant at 4am. Everyone waiting for first train. Pasta in silence.
Everyone ends up here. Every major station. The unspoken rule.
Gyudon at 3am. ¥400. Fills the void immediately.
Standing soba at 2am. ¥500. Pure and functional.
Tiny legendary sardine broth ramen. Queue even at 2am.
Yuzu shio ramen. Clean, bright broth. Open late on weekends.
Private booths, shower, manga wall to the ceiling. ¥1500-2500 overnight.
Flat-bed booths available, shower. Solid overnight option.
Well-maintained. Flat beds, shower. Better than average.
Sleep rooms not just booths. ¥1500-2000 overnight.
Basement after-hours. Opens as clubs close. Runs until sunrise.
Shimokitazawa bar running absurdly late. Retro game aesthetic, tiny space.
A handful of Golden-Gai bars stay until 5am. Find the ones still lit.
The Sankaku Chitai triangle keeps going until first train.
Unmarked basement speakeasy. Stays open late on its own schedule.
Not a bar. Just walk Kabukicho at 2-3am. The city at its strangest.
200+ micro bars in 6 alleys. Each is someone's obsession. Walk until one calls to you.

ハモニカ横丁 · yokocho cluster · tiny bars/food stalls · maze-like evening food cluster · Map as covered-ish/narrow arcade cluster for night mode. · Food-focused; avoid treating this as a drinking recommendation.
Memory Lane — smoky, postwar, yakitori counter. The most atmospheric alley in Tokyo.
三角地帯 · yokocho cluster · tiny bars/izakaya · maze-like alley dinner crawl · Map as cluster; this is the point of Sangenjaya nights. · Food-focused; avoid treating this as a drinking recommendation.
えびす横丁 · yokocho cluster · izakaya cluster · chaotic yokocho dinner crawl · Map as cluster; ideal night energy, age caution. · Food-focused; avoid treating this as a drinking recommendation.
Postwar izakayas under the elevated line. Yakitori smoke and salary workers in suits.
Old shitamachi drinking culture. Best atmospheric izakayas in Tokyo.
Dozens of indie bars, live music basements, cave spaces. The anti-Shibuya.
Small scene, regulars-only energy. Finding a good one takes luck and wandering.
Former geisha district. Alleys too narrow for cars. French bistros alongside traditional izakayas.
Vintage tram car as a bar. Absinthe fountains. Seats 12, reservation worth it.
Pharmacist-bartender grows his own botanicals, grinds them tableside. Nothing else like it.
300+ rare Japanese whisky bottles. Owner spent 20 years building this.
Genuine mid-century tiki bar. Mai tais and carved mugs.
Hidden behind a wall of green lockers in Ebisu. Ring the buzzer.
Theatrical and romantic. Good for a significant moment.
Every drink ¥300. In Ginza. This is the loophole.
Intimate jazz bar. Live sets some nights.
Eight-seat counter. Seasonal omakase cocktails. Reserve ahead.
Oslo coffee shop by day, cocktail bar by night. Excellent taste throughout.
Shimokitazawa basement bar. Reggae and jazz, impossibly small space.
Golden Gai's most vertical bar. Three floors of five people each, all strangers.
Kirin craft taproom in Daikanyama. Smart industrial space, seasonal batches.
Craft beer beside the canal. Small batch, seasonal.
Shinjuku 2-chome. The most welcoming bar in Tokyo. Everyone belongs here.
Owner plays live piano while you drink. Bizarre, intimate, wonderful.
Counter-only. 50+ fillings made to order. Queue expected and worth it.
Loach hot pot since 1801. One of the oldest dining experiences in Tokyo.
キッチン ミキ · student yoshoku · yoshoku/set meal · old student lunch plate · Good Waseda everyday cheap lunch.
Thai curry in Waseda since the 1980s. Cult following, tiny space.
Invented the anpan in 1874. Imperial warrant. The original.
The great tonkatsu counter. Open since 1939. Line moves fast.
Tsukemen that changed the game. Intense bonito-forward broth.
Chicken paitan ramen. Silky, rich, healing. Small counter.
Fish market vendors restocking, sushi bars opening at 6am. Best breakfast in Tokyo.
Stand at a counter under the Yamanote line, smoke everywhere. As Tokyo as it gets.
The gold standard basement food hall. A full day's entertainment underground.
Ramen Street below the station. Worth the queue for the thick-broth tsukemen.
Beloved local deli. The neighborhood goes here.
Hand-drip, thick toast, owner who has been there 40 years. Very slow, very good.
Gyudon chain, ¥380. Open all night. Honest.
Most legendary fruit shop in Japan. Since 1834. A ¥10,000 melon is the point.
Imperial family supplier for 500 years. The yokan will make you reconsider sugar.
Kagurazaka wagashi. Monaka filled to order at the counter.
The original sakura mochi since 1717. Mukojima by the Sumida. No other reason to be there.
Ikebukuro station-area custard apple pie; already fits your sweets gap.
Gift-tier wagashi. The packaging is half the experience.
Modern wagashi tea salon. Contemporary Japanese design, seasonal menu.
Seasonal fresh wagashi. Each piece is a miniature landscape of the current month.
Byzantine cathedral, gold iconostasis. 1891. Entirely wrong for the neighborhood.
Kenzo Tange's 1964 stainless steel crown. Stands over the neighborhood like a spacecraft.
Tange's 1964 Olympics masterpiece. Stand under the suspended roof and look up.
Architecture as gift to a neighborhood. The best bookshop in Tokyo. Open until midnight.
Frank Lloyd Wright's 1921 building. Prairie style, perfect human scale.
Transplanted Edo-period buildings, including the bathhouse that became Spirited Away.
Tadao Ando building buried in the ground. Design exhibitions that actually reward attention.
Contemporary art on the 53rd floor with the city below it.
Tokyo University natural history collection in a repurposed 1930s building. Free and strange.
1914 red-brick station, perfectly restored in 2012. Stand in the dome ceiling inside.
The mall that stayed genuinely otaku while Akihabara got touristy. Mandarake floors.
Thai, Nepalese, halal butchers, mosque, Korean grocery. Genuinely multicultural mile.
Moomin theme forest in Saitama. Genuinely magical if you commit to it.
Kengo Kuma building, ancient Japanese art, bamboo garden. The quiet alternative.
Former geisha district. Alleys too narrow for cars. The most European kilometer in Tokyo.
The crossing from above at night. Unobstructed city view. Worth the ¥2000.
The last shitamachi neighborhood. Survived the earthquake and the war.
The neighborhood that stayed strange. Vintage shops, record stores, people who mean it.
Shotengai that survived the earthquake, the war, and gentrification.
The sunset staircase above Yanaka Ginza. Come at golden hour.
Soviet clocks, 1970s signage, folk art, medical equipment. One of a kind.
US Air Force base town. Rockabilly bars, military surplus. Still feels like 1962.
1970s hostess bars that never renovated. Faded curtains, handwritten menus.
Wholesale hour before the market opens. Fish vendors, produce stalls restocking.
The basement food hall alone is 90 minutes. Find the cheese counter, the lacquerware floor.
Village Vanguard, Baroque, dozens of vintage shops. The thrift pilgrimage.
After 9pm: neon-lit, half-empty, arcades and rogue electronics.
The world's densest used bookstore district. Also the best vinyl hunting in Tokyo.
Eight floors of used vinyl and CDs. Staff know exactly what you're looking for.
Traditional strolling garden 5 min walk. Free. Almost nobody goes.
Hotel garden you can walk into free. Fireflies in June.
Massive and undervisited. The ginkgo tunnel in November is unforgettable.
The city's exhale. Picnics, musicians, dogs, people doing what they want.
1964 Olympics venue. Brutalist towers, open grass, almost no tourists.
The dark park that never closes. What lives here at 3am is its own world.
Torii tunnel predating Fushimi Inari by two centuries. Come before 8am.
Oldest temple in Tokyo. The soba shops that circle it are the secondary attraction.
The man-made forest is now 100 years old and feels ancient. Silence inside.
Origin of the beckoning cat. Hundreds of maneki-neko statues.
Red shrine in Shinjuku. Holds its own against the surrounding neon. Open late.
Escalator up the hill through a hundred vermilion torii gates.
Before 6am it belongs to the monks and vendors setting up. A completely different place.
Buddhist temple with Tokyo Tower backdrop. Better than expected, especially at night.
First train ~5:30am. Summit by sunrise. Almost alone up there.
100 foxes roaming free in a mountain forest. Genuinely surreal.
Rabbit Island. Hundreds of rabbits, WWII poison gas factory ruins.
2hrs from Shinjuku. Real mountains, deep valley. Genuinely unexpected.
Meiji-era coastal fort on the only Tokyo Bay island without a bridge. Ferry only.
Roman-themed onsen. Columns, mosaics, milk bath options. Not subtle.
Goldfish as art. Projection, illuminated tanks, thousands of fish.
¥500. Rotenburo under the city sky. Split up, meet in the lobby.
Waterside dessert/cafe option near Iidabashi/Kagurazaka.
Projected stars above your drinks. Quiet and surprisingly good.
Walk barefoot through water and light installations. Book ahead.
Cherry blossom tunnel in spring. Cafe-lined in any season. Good at night too.
Views to Fuji on clear days. The old Oshiage streets below are better than the tower.
Fake beach, Rainbow Bridge, city glow across the bay. Artificial and weirdly romantic.
French garden in autumn rain is quietly perfect.
Legend: couples who row together break up. The goddess is real.
Outdoor bath with Fuji view. The canonical Tokyo couples overnight.
Taisho-era stone lantern village. Snow in winter. Magical.
Giant Buddha, hydrangea paths, sea views. The canonical day trip.
Toshogu shrine complex in forest. Gongen architecture. 2hrs from Ueno.
Little Edo warehouse district. Penny candy alley since Meiji era.
Best-preserved Edo post town. Unspoiled street for 1km.
Causeway island with caves, shrine, sea views, and cats. Easy from Shinjuku.
Mountain town with its own soba, Ichiro's Malt whisky distillery, real festival calendar.
Perry's black ships landed here. Tropical port at the tip of Izu Peninsula.
The art island. Chichu Museum, Benesse House, Yayoi Kusama. Take the ferry.
Petrochemical plants lit up, reflected in the bay. Blade Runner. Take a night bus.
Entire abandoned company town — apartments, hospital, school. 2hrs from Tokyo.
Meiji-era coastal fort on an island in Tokyo Bay. No bridge. Ferry from Yokosuka.
Apartments designed to disorient — lumpy floors, sphere rooms. Tour by appointment.
Old-school multi-floor bakery and kissaten; dependable cakes, parfaits, and morning sets.
Zodiac-themed parfaits and very 1980s Tokyo dessert energy.
Use Tobu and Seibu basements as the easy sweets answer when everyone wants different things.
Specialty coffee near the east side; good low-friction reset stop.
Bakery-cafe useful for morning, coffee, and a civilized sit-down.
Historic wagashi shop known for kakigori and seasonal Japanese sweets.
